key.reminder: Screwtape is a devil, so his “good advice” is upside down. What he wants is bad for the person, and what he calls bad is good.

terms.def: Screwtape’s nephew, a junior tempter in training.

chapter_00.mp3.line: Lewis, with a straight face, says these are real letters between two devils, and warns that people make two opposite mistakes about devils: ignoring them completely, or getting unhealthily fascinated by them.

vocab.def: having bad intentions; spiteful.
Lewis’s reminder that Screwtape twists the truth even inside his own letters, so not everything he says is reliable.

chapter_00.mp3.today: Read these knowing the narrator is dishonest. Both the humor and the warning come from flipping his advice right-side up.
chapter_00.mp3.ask: Am I more tempted to ignore evil entirely, or to be fascinated by it?

chapter_01.mp3.line: Screwtape tells his nephew not to fight for the man’s soul with argument. People no longer decide what is true by careful reasoning, so the smarter play is to keep him glued to “ordinary life” and clever-sounding words, and never let him stop to think.

vocab.def: naive; innocent in a way Screwtape finds laughable.
someone who believes only the physical world is real, and nothing spiritual.
to muddle or confuse. “You are there to fuddle him.”

chapter_01.mp3.context: The British Museum in London housed a vast public Reading Room, a hushed and serious place where people went to study for hours. The story lands because that is exactly the kind of quiet setting where a man might follow an idea all the way to God, which is why Screwtape is so eager to get his atheist out of it and onto the noisy street.
chapter_01.mp3.today: We still get talked out of faith less by arguments and more by busyness and noise. A full calendar, a loud feed, and the steady pull of “real life” can keep a person from ever sitting still long enough to ask the big questions.
chapter_01.mp3.ask: What in my day keeps me too busy, or too distracted, to think honestly about God?

chapter_02.mp3.line: The man has just become a Christian. Screwtape says do not panic; instead use his disappointment with the actual church, and with the dull, ordinary people in the next pew, to sour him on the whole thing.

vocab.def: the deflating letdown when reality fails to live up to the buildup.

chapter_02.mp3.today: New faith often collides with a very ordinary church full of flawed, unimpressive people. That gap between the dream and the reality is where a lot of people quietly give up.
chapter_02.mp3.ask: When church or other Christians let me down, do I let it pull me away from God himself?

chapter_03.mp3.line: The man lives with his mother and they wear on each other. Screwtape coaches Wormwood to weaponize the daily friction: tones of voice, small irritations, and the gap between the saintly “mother” the man prays for and the real woman across the breakfast table.

vocab.term: the elder brother in the Enemy’s story
vocab.def: the resentful older brother in Jesus’s parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15), who is angry at grace given to someone else.

chapter_03.mp3.today: The people we live with get our worst selves. Small tones and daily friction do more quiet spiritual damage than the occasional big blowup.
chapter_03.mp3.ask: Who gets the worst version of me at home, and why do I let them?

chapter_04.mp3.line: Prayer. Screwtape wants to keep the man away from real, honest prayer by steering him toward vague feelings and self-generated “moods” instead of actually turning to God.

vocab.def: feelings a person works up inside himself and mistakes for prayer, instead of really addressing God.

chapter_04.mp3.today: Prayer can quietly become a feeling we try to manufacture rather than a real conversation with a real God.
chapter_04.mp3.ask: Is my prayer actually aimed at God, or just a mood I am trying to produce?

chapter_05.mp3.line: War has broken out and Wormwood is thrilled. Screwtape warns him not to get drunk on it. Suffering and death do not automatically deliver souls to Hell, and a fresh nearness of death can drive people straight to God.
chapter_05.mp3.context: This is the Second World War, just begun for Britain. Screwtape’s cold point is that human suffering only helps Hell if it produces sin, and that war makes people remember death, which is bad for Hell’s long game.
chapter_05.mp3.today: A crisis can wake people up to God as easily as it can harden them. Hard times are not automatically faith-killers.
chapter_05.mp3.ask: When life turns frightening, does it tend to push me toward God or away from him?

chapter_06.mp3.line: Screwtape teaches Wormwood how to handle the man’s wartime anxiety: keep his ill will aimed at the people right around him, and keep his kindness vague and aimed at distant people he never actually has to love.

vocab.def: weak, timid, spineless people.

chapter_06.mp3.today: It is easy to “love humanity” in general while being cold and short with the actual people in the room.
chapter_06.mp3.ask: Is my kindness real and up close, or vague and conveniently far away?

chapter_07.mp3.line: Should the man become a fierce patriot or a fierce pacifist? Screwtape does not care which, as long as the cause becomes more important to him than God, and his faith curdles into a faction.

vocab.def: turning the faith into “Christianity and patriotism,” or “Christianity and pacifism,” until the add-on quietly matters more than Christ.

chapter_07.mp3.context: Pacifism was a live and heated argument in wartime Britain, which is why Screwtape uses it as his example. Either side works for him.
chapter_07.mp3.today: Any good cause can slowly take God’s place, until the cause is the real religion and Jesus is just its mascot.
chapter_07.mp3.ask: Is there a cause I have let grow more important to me than God himself?

chapter_08.mp3.line: Screwtape explains the “Law of Undulation”: humans naturally rise and fall between highs and dry, flat lows. The low stretches are not failures, and God does some of his deepest work in them.

vocab.def: a rising and falling, like waves; the natural up-and-down of human energy and mood.
the low point between two waves; the flat, dry seasons of life.

chapter_08.mp3.today: Spiritual dry spells are normal, not proof that something has gone wrong. God is often closest in the flat seasons.
chapter_08.mp3.ask: How do I treat the dry, unfelt stretches of faith: as failure, or as normal weather?

chapter_09.mp3.line: Screwtape shows how to exploit the man’s low period: feed him easy pleasures, and convince him his whole religious “phase” was just a mood that has now safely passed.

vocab.def: the low, flat period (see Letter VIII).
reaching for physical comfort and pleasure to numb the dryness.

chapter_09.mp3.today: When faith feels flat, we reach for easy comfort, and start telling ourselves it was probably never real anyway.
chapter_09.mp3.ask: When I feel spiritually empty, what do I reach for to fill the gap?

chapter_10.mp3.line: The man falls in with a worldly, clever, fashionable crowd. Screwtape is delighted: let him enjoy being admired, and let him slowly become a phonier version of himself to fit in.

vocab.def: a sophisticated, cynical social circle that treats faith as embarrassing and unfashionable.

chapter_10.mp3.today: We quietly reshape ourselves to be liked by whatever room we are in, and can lose track of who we actually are.
chapter_10.mp3.ask: Do I become a different person to fit in with certain people?

chapter_11.mp3.line: Screwtape breaks laughter into four kinds and names the one Hell loves best: flippancy, the habit of treating everything as a joke so that nothing is ever taken seriously.

vocab.def: cheap, constant mockery that treats every serious thing as a joke.
genuine humor about real incongruity, which Screwtape actually dislikes.

chapter_11.mp3.today: There is a kind of nonstop sarcasm that slowly makes a person unable to take anything, or anyone, seriously.
chapter_11.mp3.ask: Do I use humor to connect with people, or to keep everything at a safe, mocking distance?

chapter_12.mp3.line: Screwtape describes the slow drift: small, vague, unconfessed sins that gradually dull the man’s love for God until he barely notices how far off he has wandered.
chapter_12.mp3.today: Most people do not walk away from God in one big decision. They drift, in small steps they never quite notice. As Lewis has Screwtape put it, the safest road to Hell is the gradual one.
chapter_12.mp3.ask: Is there a slow drift in my life that I have simply stopped noticing?

chapter_13.mp3.line: The man has had a real moment, a book he honestly loved and a quiet walk he truly enjoyed, and it has reconnected him to God. Screwtape is alarmed and wants Wormwood to make him self-conscious and undo it.

vocab.def: genuine, simple joys that put a person back in touch with reality, and so with God.
two pennies; to “not care twopence” is to not care at all what others think (an old British coin).

chapter_13.mp3.today: Honest, simple delights, a real book or a real walk, can pull us back toward God better than forced religious effort ever does.
chapter_13.mp3.ask: When did I last enjoy something simply and honestly, without performing it for anyone?

chapter_14.mp3.line: The man is becoming genuinely humble. Screwtape’s countermove is to make him proud of his humility, and to keep his attention fixed on himself, because God wants him to forget himself entirely.

vocab.def: forgetting yourself entirely, rather than running yourself down.

chapter_14.mp3.today: The moment we notice that we are being humble, we usually are not. Real humility forgets itself.
chapter_14.mp3.ask: Am I more focused on how I am doing spiritually than on God himself?

chapter_15.mp3.line: Screwtape on time. Keep the man living in the Future, with its worry and daydreams, or in the Past, because God wants him in the Present, the only place where he can actually meet God.

vocab.def: tormented and worn out, as if ridden all night by a witch; here, consumed by anxiety about the future.

chapter_15.mp3.today: We spend the present anxious about the future or replaying the past, and miss the one moment where God is actually available to us.
chapter_15.mp3.ask: How much of today am I losing to worry about tomorrow?

chapter_16.mp3.line: Screwtape turns the man into a church-shopper and a sermon-critic, always hunting for a congregation that suits his taste, so that he never settles, never belongs, and never grows.

vocab.def: an expert critic with refined taste; here, of churches and sermons, as if shopping for the best one.
minor, non-essential matters (candles, styles, wording) that Screwtape inflates into dividing lines.

chapter_16.mp3.today: Treating church like a product to sample and rate keeps us as critics on the outside instead of members on the inside.
chapter_16.mp3.ask: Do I come to church as a consumer judging a performance, or as a member giving myself to it?

chapter_17.mp3.line: Gluttony, but not the obvious kind. Screwtape praises the “gluttony of Delicacy,” the fussy, never-satisfied “all I want is…” that disguises real self-centeredness as having small, modest needs.

vocab.def: being endlessly picky and demanding, especially about food, while feeling virtuously modest about it.
constant peevish complaining.

chapter_17.mp3.today: Selfishness loves to dress up as having simple, refined preferences. “I just want it done right” can run a whole household ragged.
chapter_17.mp3.ask: Do my “small preferences” quietly make life harder for the people around me?

chapter_18.mp3.line: Marriage and love. Screwtape lays out Hell’s entire philosophy, that all of life is competition and one creature only gains by feeding on another, and applies it to the feeling of “being in love.”

vocab.def: the romantic feeling, which Screwtape hopes the man treats as the whole point of marriage, so he bails out when it fades.

chapter_18.mp3.today: If love is only a feeling, it dies when the feeling fades. Real love is also a promise that outlasts the mood.
chapter_18.mp3.ask: Do I treat love mainly as a feeling I have, or a commitment I keep?

chapter_19.mp3.line: Screwtape gets nervous. He half-admits that Hell still cannot figure out why God genuinely loves humans, then scrambles to take it back, all while pushing Wormwood to exploit the man’s new romance.
chapter_19.mp3.today: Even the enemy in the story cannot explain God’s love. It simply does not compute in a world built on pure self-interest, and that is exactly the point.
chapter_19.mp3.ask: Do I actually believe God loves me, or do I only hope it?

chapter_20.mp3.line: Sexual temptation through “taste.” Screwtape explains how Hell shapes what people find desirable, steering a man’s longing toward an ideal image that does not really exist.

vocab.def: how much desire gets driven by looking, and by images, rather than by a real person.
earthly sexual desire (Venus is the Roman goddess of love), as opposed to higher kinds of love.

chapter_20.mp3.today: A great deal of desire is aimed at images and ideals that no real person can match, which leaves people endlessly restless.
chapter_20.mp3.ask: Are my desires shaped by real people, or by images that do not exist?

chapter_21.mp3.line: Screwtape teaches peevishness about “my time” and “my things.” The man feels he owns his day, so every interruption feels like theft, when in truth all of it is a gift.

vocab.def: a private one-on-one conversation (French, literally “head to head”).
the false sense that our hours belong to us by right.

chapter_21.mp3.today: We treat our time as a possession, so interruptions feel like robbery. Everything we have, including the next hour, is on loan.
chapter_21.mp3.ask: When my plans get interrupted, why do I react as if something were stolen from me?

chapter_22.mp3.line: The man falls in love with a genuinely good Christian woman, and her warm home and family quietly undo much of Screwtape’s work. Screwtape flies into such a rage that he accidentally turns himself into a centipede.

vocab.def: Hell’s program of constant distraction. Screwtape says Hell means to make the whole universe into noise, and that he hates music and silence.

chapter_22.mp3.context: The bracketed line, “Here the MS breaks off and is resumed in a different hand,” is an editor’s note. In his fury Screwtape transformed mid-letter, and his secretary Toadpipe finished writing it for him.
chapter_22.mp3.today: A loving, ordinary Christian home is quietly one of the most powerful good influences there is.
chapter_22.mp3.ask: Who are the people and places that quietly make me better, and am I near them enough?

chapter_23.mp3.line: Screwtape pushes the “historical Jesus”: scholarly reconstructions that swap the living Christ for a figure each generation invents, so the man ends up worshiping an idea instead of a person.

vocab.def: scholarly attempts to dig out the “real” Jesus behind the Gospels, which tend to produce a Jesus who conveniently matches the scholar’s own views.

chapter_23.mp3.today: It is possible to study Jesus endlessly and never actually meet him, trading the living Christ for a theory about him.
chapter_23.mp3.ask: Am I relating to Jesus as a real, present person, or just as an idea I have opinions about?

chapter_24.mp3.line: Screwtape works on spiritual pride. Let the man’s good new Christian circle make him quietly smug, conceited about belonging to the “right,” enlightened set.

vocab.def: feeling superior to others because of your faith or your particular group.

chapter_24.mp3.today: Few things sour real growth faster than starting to feel spiritually superior to other people.
chapter_24.mp3.ask: Has my faith ever made me feel better than other people instead of more humble?

chapter_25.mp3.line: Screwtape on “the horror of the Same Old Thing,” the craving for novelty. He wants the man hooked on newness for its own sake, restless with anything familiar, including steady faith.

vocab.def: Screwtape’s name for ordinary faithfulness, which he tries to make feel boring so the man always craves the next new thing.

chapter_25.mp3.today: A constant hunger for the new can make ordinary faithfulness feel unbearable, when faithfulness is exactly the point.
chapter_25.mp3.ask: Do I confuse “new” with “good,” and grow bored with steady, faithful things?

chapter_26.mp3.line: Screwtape weaponizes “unselfishness.” In the couple’s overly polite deferring, the endless “no, you choose,” he manufactures buried resentment that poses as courtesy.

vocab.def: a fake, self-congratulating deference that secretly keeps score and breeds resentment.

chapter_26.mp3.today: Endless “no, whatever you want” can be less about love and more about dodging blame while quietly resenting the other person.
chapter_26.mp3.ask: Is my “unselfishness” real generosity, or a way to feel noble and keep score?

chapter_27.mp3.line: Prayer again, plus Screwtape’s frustration. He arms the man with clever objections to asking God for things, then admits that Hell still cannot crack why God does what he does.

vocab.def: prayer that actually asks God for things.
Screwtape’s joke name for Hell’s spying on God’s plans, which keeps failing.

chapter_27.mp3.today: Clever arguments against prayer can quietly talk us out of ever actually asking God for anything.
chapter_27.mp3.ask: Have I reasoned myself out of simply asking God for what I need?

chapter_28.mp3.line: Air raids are coming, and surprisingly Screwtape wants the man kept alive, not killed. Dying now, in faith, would send him to God, while long comfortable years give Hell far more time to work.
chapter_28.mp3.context: The air raids are the German bombing of British cities, the Blitz. Screwtape’s cold logic is that a sudden death in faith means Heaven, so Hell would rather have the slow erosion of a long, easy life.
chapter_28.mp3.today: Time and comfort can do slow spiritual damage that crisis never could. A long, easy life is not automatically a safe one.
chapter_28.mp3.ask: Am I letting comfortable years quietly drift me away from God?

chapter_29.mp3.line: With danger close, Screwtape turns to fear and courage. He wants the man to become a coward and then to excuse it, because courage is the form every other virtue takes at its testing point.
chapter_29.mp3.today: Courage is not just one virtue among many. It is the point where every other virtue becomes real. Fear handled badly quietly corrodes all of them.
chapter_29.mp3.ask: Where is fear secretly steering my choices?

chapter_30.mp3.line: Worn down by fear and fatigue, the man is tempted to call the wartime horror “real life” and his faith “unreal.” Screwtape wants exhaustion itself to redefine what counts as true.

vocab.def: Screwtape’s trick of making grim, ugly things feel more real and true than good and holy ones.

chapter_30.mp3.today: When we are tired and afraid, the bleakest version of life starts to feel like the only true one. Exhaustion is a liar about reality.
chapter_30.mp3.ask: When I am worn out, what starts to feel “real” that may not be the whole truth?

chapter_31.mp3.line: The man dies in an air raid and goes straight to God. Screwtape’s final letter drops all its charm into pure fury and despair as he loses the soul for good.

vocab.def: mock terms of endearment Screwtape uses at the opening before his tone collapses into rage. (“Pigsnie” is an old word for a darling.)

chapter_31.mp3.today: The whole long battle ends in a single moment of grace. From the believer’s side, death is not the enemy’s victory. It is the homecoming.
chapter_31.mp3.ask: Do I live as if death is the end of the story, or the doorway in it?

toast.mp3.line: Years later, Screwtape gives an after-dinner speech to a graduating class of young tempters. His complaint is that the quality of damned souls has gone downhill, and he explains how a leveling, “I’m as good as anyone” culture quietly mass-produces small, mediocre, easily tempted people.

vocab.def: Screwtape’s name for resentful equality, envy dressed up as fairness, which he loves.
he hijacks the word to mean not a form of government but a mood that hates to see anyone be better at anything.

toast.mp3.context: Dr. Slubgob is the principal of the Tempters’ Training College, Screwtape’s host at the dinner. The “Pharisee wine” is the joke that Screwtape savors the souls of damned hypocrites like fine vintage wine; smug, self-righteous religious people make the richest meal in Hell. (The Pharisees were the religious hypocrites Jesus rebuked.)
toast.mp3.today: A culture that resents excellence and flattens everyone to the same level does not lift people up. It quietly drags them down, and congratulates itself for being fair.
toast.mp3.ask: Do I celebrate goodness and excellence in others, or quietly resent it?
